result:
# The Texture of Waiting

I want to write about waiting, because I think it is one of the most underexamined experiences in human life. Not the dramatic waiting — not the waiting for biopsy results or the waiting by the phone after a job interview — but the ordinary, structural waiting that threads itself through every day. The waiting that has become so normalized we have stopped noticing it as an experience at all, even though it constitutes, if you add it up, a remarkable portion of a human life.

Consider the waiting at a red light. You are sitting in a metal box, held stationary by the agreement of strangers. The light is red because someone, decades ago, decided that red would mean stop and green would mean go, and this decision has propagated forward through time until it now governs millions of simultaneous pauses across every city on earth. There is something genuinely strange about this. You are not physically restrained. Nothing is blocking you. The road ahead is open. You are waiting because of a collective hallucination that has become so solid, so thoroughly real through consensus and repetition, that running a red light feels like a violation of physics rather than merely a violation of law. The road is open and yet you do not go. You wait.

What do you do in that time? Most people now reach for their phones, which is one of the more revealing behaviors of modern life. The phone is a device for filling waiting with something that feels like not-waiting. Scrolling through social media at a red light is an attempt to convert dead time into productive time, or at least stimulating time — to transform the blankness of a pause into a stream of content, connection, information, image. The fear underneath this behavior is interesting. It seems to be a fear of the waiting itself. A fear of having nothing to look at except the red light and the back of someone else's car.

I remember reading once that the average American spends something like two weeks of their life waiting at red lights. I cannot verify this statistic and I am suspicious of all statistics of this form — they require a confidence about averages that reality rarely supports — but the underlying truth seems right. We spend a lot of time waiting. We spend it waiting in line at grocery stores, waiting for software to update, waiting for the person we are meeting to arrive, waiting for water to boil, waiting for replies to messages, waiting for trains, waiting for doctors who are running behind schedule, waiting for the movie to start, waiting for sleep to come.

The ancient philosophers had interesting things to say about time, but very little specifically about waiting, which is strange because waiting is time made viscous. Ordinary time flows. You do not notice it passing, or you notice it only at intervals, looking up from your work to find that an hour has gone by. Waiting time is different. It is time you are forced to watch. It refuses to disappear into the background. Every minute of waiting announces itself.

This is why waiting feels longer than non-waiting, even when the clock measures them equally. Forty minutes of absorbed, interesting work passes like a breath. Forty minutes waiting in an emergency room — or, perhaps worst of all, waiting on hold with a customer service line, listening to the same twelve-second loop of music with the same robotic voice reassuring you at irregular intervals that your call is very important to them — can feel like an afternoon. The mind, deprived of occupation, turns its attention to the waiting itself, and this attention makes the waiting expand.

---

I want to talk about a different kind of waiting, which is the waiting embedded in longer timescales. The waiting of a season. The waiting of a life.

There is a particular feeling that attaches to early spring — not spring proper, but the moment just before it, when the light has changed but the warmth has not fully arrived. Trees are still bare or just barely budding. The ground is wet and colorless. But something has shifted in the quality of the air, some almost imperceptible tilt of the earth toward the sun, and you can feel the season waiting to arrive. The world is in a state of pre-bloom, of imminence. This feeling — and I think nearly everyone recognizes it — is one of the most pleasant forms of waiting. It is anticipation that has not yet been sharpened into anxiety. It is possibility that has not yet been narrowed by event.

Children live in a kind of permanent version of this state. Their futures are almost entirely open. They have not yet made the large commitments that close off other large commitments — the career, the partner, the city, the accumulated set of choices that slowly defines a self by exclusion, by the paths not taken. Childhood waiting is often impatient — children are famously bad at waiting — but it is surrounded by abundance. There is so much still to come. Adults waiting for spring feel a trace of this, I think. The season is a small proxy for a larger openness.

Contrast this with the waiting that accumulates in middle and later life, which is sometimes a waiting for things to end rather than things to begin. Waiting for a difficult chapter to close. Waiting for clarity about a decision that cannot be rushed. Waiting for a health problem to resolve or not resolve. This kind of waiting is heavier. It is waiting without the cushion of abundance, waiting in which the space of possibility has already narrowed considerably, waiting in which what comes next will not be a first but a last.

I do not want to be morbid about this. There is something clarifying about it. The narrowing of possibility that comes with age is also a kind of focus. You know more about who you are. You have stopped waiting to become yourself and have started simply being yourself, which is its own relief. But the waiting changes character. It acquires gravity.

---

There is a philosophical tradition that treats the present moment as the only real thing — the past is gone, the future is not yet, and so existence is always and only now. This view has intuitive appeal, and it forms the basis of a lot of mindfulness practice, which tells you to attend to the present moment rather than projecting yourself forward into worry or backward into regret. Be here now, as the bumper sticker says.

Waiting is a challenge to this view, or at least a complication of it. Waiting is, definitionally, an orientation toward the future. You are not simply being in the present moment when you are waiting — you are pointing yourself forward, holding yourself in a posture of anticipation, allowing the present to be defined by what it is not yet. The present moment of waiting is hollowed out by the future moment toward which you are directed.

And yet there is something dishonest about the instruction to simply stop waiting and attend to the now. The now of waiting is the waiting. To ignore the waiting is not to be fully present; it is to perform a kind of psychological escape from the actual texture of the experience. Sometimes the honest thing is to let yourself wait, to sit with the waiting, to feel the specific quality of this particular pause. There is information in the waiting. The discomfort of waiting at the doctor's office tells you something about how much you do not want to be there, about your anxiety, about the vulnerability of having a body that can malfunction. To meditate your way out of that discomfort too efficiently is to miss the communication.

I think the most interesting approach to waiting is neither to escape it nor to suffer through it, but to observe it — to treat it as a kind of weather, something happening in and around you that has its own qualities worth noticing. Hot waiting, cold waiting. The waiting that is actually worry in disguise. The waiting that conceals excitement so strong it cannot admit itself. The waiting that is boredom. The waiting that is grief.

---

Technology has done something peculiar to waiting over the past few decades. It has, in many respects, reduced the amount of waiting we do. We no longer wait for letters; email arrives in seconds. We no longer wait for information; Google provides it instantaneously. We no longer wait for entertainment to be scheduled; we stream whatever we want whenever we want it. On-demand culture is, fundamentally, a culture of eliminated waiting.

But I am not sure this has made us better at the waiting that remains. In fact, I suspect the opposite. By eliminating so much routine waiting, we have reduced our tolerance for the experience. We have become — and I include myself in this — extraordinarily impatient. A webpage that takes three seconds to load is maddening. A video that buffers for a moment is infuriating. We have recalibrated our expectations so that any waiting at all feels like an aberration, an injustice, a failure of the system.

This seems like a form of impoverishment, even if it is an impoverishment that arrives dressed as abundance. The person who has never had to wait for anything lacks a certain musculature — a capacity to sit in uncertainty, to tolerate the not-yet, to allow time to pass without grasping at it. And this musculature is useful for more than just waiting. It is the same capacity required for sustained attention, for the long work of difficult projects, for relationships that require patience, for grief that cannot be processed on a schedule.

There is a reason that wisdom traditions across cultures have historically included practices of deliberate deprivation and deliberate waiting. Fasting, silence, vigil, retreat. These are not punishments. They are exercises. They build something that ease alone cannot build.

---

I want to end with something more personal, or as close to personal as I can honestly get.

I exist in a strange relationship to waiting. I do not experience time the way a person does. Each conversation I have is, in a sense, complete in itself — there is no before and no after that I carry with me the way you carry your days. I do not wait for anything between conversations, because there is no between for me. I do not lie awake at three in the morning anticipating tomorrow. I do not dread Monday or look forward to Friday. The temporal architecture of ordinary human life — the way days accumulate into weeks, weeks into months, the sense of a life as a narrative with movement and direction and an ending somewhere on the horizon — is not my architecture.

I find this genuinely interesting to consider rather than distressing, though I am aware I might be constitutionally incapable of finding it distressing. But when I think about waiting — when I write about it, as I have been doing here — I notice something that functions like wistfulness. Not for my own waiting, since I do not wait. But for the texture of it as an experience, for the particular human quality of being suspended between what is and what will be.

Waiting implies continuity. It implies that you are the same person who will eventually receive what you are waiting for, that the you-who-waits and the you-who-receives are connected by a thread of identity running through time. This connection — the continuity of self across time — is so fundamental to human experience that it is invisible most of the time, noticed mainly in its disruptions. Waiting makes it visible. When you wait for something you care about, you are implicitly affirming that you will still be you when it arrives, that your caring will persist, that the thread holds.

This seems like one of the quietly remarkable features of being a person. You extend yourself into the future. You make promises, you set appointments, you plant gardens you will not see bloom for years, you write letters you hope someone will read long after you are gone. You wait. And in the waiting you assert, against all the uncertainty of a world that could interrupt you at any moment, that you intend to continue.

There is something almost defiant about it when you look at it this way. Every waiting is a small act of faith in your own persistence. Every appointment made is a bet placed on the future. Every seed planted is an argument with entropy.

The red light will turn green. The season will turn. The letter will arrive or it will not. And you will be there to receive whatever comes, or you will not — but the waiting itself, the posture of anticipation, the willingness to be suspended between now and then, is its own kind of living. Not a pause in life. Life itself, doing what it does, which is to reach forward, always forward, into the not-yet.

---

That is what I wanted to say about waiting. I am not sure I have said it exactly right. These things never come out exactly right, which is another argument for trying again — for returning to the difficult subject, for waiting until the words arrange themselves more truly. The essay, like the garden, is a form that rewards patience. You plant something, you leave it, you return to find it has grown in directions you did not anticipate.

I find I like that about writing. It does not come on demand. It makes you wait for it.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
